title: Recon Script Builder
learning_objectives: Build a modular Bash recon script with professional structure
Implement argument parsing, validation, and help functions
Generate clean engagement reports from CLI tools
key_topics: Report assembly from multiple tool outputs
scenario: Create a modular Bash recon script that your team can use on any engagement. It should accept a target domain and run a standard set of passive checks, outputting a clean report.

scenario_context.challenge: Build a professional-grade Bash recon script
scenario_context.relevance: Bash scripts are the backbone of offensive automation on Linux. A well-structured script saves hours on every engagement.

subtasks.title: Script Framework
subtasks.description: Create a script with argument validation, help message (-h flag), target domain variable, and output directory creation with timestamp.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Start with #!/bin/bash. Use getopts for -t target and -h help. Validate input. Create timestamped output directory. Add colour output if you want extra credit.
subtasks.evidence_required: Working script framework with help function

subtasks.title: Recon Functions
subtasks.description: Add functions for WHOIS lookup, DNS enumeration (dig), subdomain check (host), and output formatting.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Each function should: take target as parameter, run the command, capture output, save to file in output directory. Use descriptive function names.

subtasks.title: Report Assembly
subtasks.description: Add a function that combines all output files into a single summary report with headers and timestamps.
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Read all output files. Create a master report with: header, timestamp, target info, then each section with clear headers. Make it readable for a client.

reflection_prompt: A teammate copies your script and runs it without the -t flag. What happens? Now imagine they run it against a target without authorisation. How do you build safeguards into shared tools?

quiz.question: getopts in Bash is used for:
quiz.explanation: getopts is the standard Bash utility for parsing command-line flags like -t target and -h help.

quiz.question: Modular functions in scripts are important because:
options: They allow individual components to be tested, reused, and maintained independently
quiz.explanation: Functions create maintainable, testable, reusable code — essential for professional tooling that teams share.

mentor_notes: Script must execute end-to-end. Check: does -h work? Does it handle missing arguments? Is the output actually useful? Code structure matters.
